parentQuestion: Transitional epithelium is found in the ___.

options: Epithelium that can change shape when stretched
Epithelium that is developing into another type
scaffolds.explain: TRANSITIONAL EPITHELIUM can CHANGE SHAPE when stretched. It transitions between appearing thick/cuboidal (relaxed) and thin/squamous (stretched).

wrongExplains.0: All epithelia can transport; transitional's special feature is shape change.
wrongExplains.2: Transitional is a mature, specialized type, not developing into another.
wrongExplains.3: 'Transitional' refers to shape change, not location.

scaffolds.q: What is the main function of the urinary bladder?
scaffolds.explain: The urinary bladder STORES URINE TEMPORARILY until elimination. It must expand significantly as it fills, requiring specialized stretchable epithelium.

wrongExplains.0: Kidneys filter blood; the bladder stores the resulting urine.
wrongExplains.2: Kidneys produce urine; the bladder stores it.
wrongExplains.3: Kidney tubules reabsorb water; the bladder just stores urine.

scaffolds.q: Why does the bladder need epithelium that can stretch?
scaffolds.explain: The bladder needs stretchable epithelium to ACCOMMODATE FILLING WITHOUT TEARING. It can hold 400-600 mL of urine, requiring significant expansion.

wrongExplains.0: The bladder stores, not filters, urine.
wrongExplains.2: The bladder doesn't absorb water; it just stores urine.
wrongExplains.3: Stretching doesn't require producing more cells.

scaffolds.q: How does transitional epithelium appear when relaxed vs. Stretched?
options: Thick and cuboidal when relaxed; thin and squamous when stretched
Thin when relaxed; thick when stretched
scaffolds.explain: When RELAXED, transitional epithelium appears THICK and CUBOIDAL (cells rounded). When STRETCHED, it becomes THIN and SQUAMOUS (cells flatten).

wrongExplains.0: Transitional epithelium changes thickness with stretch.
wrongExplains.2: It thins when stretched as cells flatten.
wrongExplains.3: It's the opposite: Thick when relaxed, thin when stretched.

options: Bladder, ureters, and part of urethra
scaffolds.explain: Transitional epithelium lines the BLADDER, URETERS, and part of the URETHRA — the urinary structures that must stretch to accommodate urine volume changes.

wrongExplains.0: Stomach and intestines have simple columnar for absorption.
wrongExplains.2: Lungs have simple squamous; blood vessels have endothelium.
wrongExplains.3: Skin has keratinized stratified squamous; mouth has non-keratinized stratified squamous.

scaffolds.q: What are dome cells (umbrella cells) in transitional epithelium?
options: Large surface cells that can flatten when stretched
scaffolds.explain: DOME CELLS (umbrella cells) are large cells on the SURFACE of transitional epithelium that can FLATTEN dramatically when the organ stretches.

wrongExplains.0: Goblet cells secrete mucus; dome cells provide stretch capability.
wrongExplains.2: Dome cells accommodate stretching; they don't sense volume.
wrongExplains.3: Basal cells regenerate epithelium; dome cells are specialized surface cells.

scaffolds.q: Why isn't simple squamous epithelium used in the bladder?
options: It's too thin to protect against urine
scaffolds.explain: Simple squamous is TOO THIN to protect against the chemical irritation of urine. Transitional epithelium provides both stretch capability AND an effective barrier.

wrongExplains.0: Simple squamous can stretch but doesn't provide adequate protection.
wrongExplains.2: The bladder shouldn't absorb water from urine.
wrongExplains.3: Mucus production isn't the key issue; protection is.

scaffolds.q: What unique property makes transitional epithelium ideal for the urinary system?
options: Ability to stretch while maintaining an impermeable barrier
scaffolds.explain: Transitional epithelium can STRETCH while maintaining an IMPERMEABLE BARRIER. This prevents urine from leaking into tissues while allowing organ expansion.

wrongExplains.0: All epithelia can divide; transitional's key feature is stretch with barrier.
wrongExplains.2: Urine moves by muscle contraction and gravity, not cilia.
wrongExplains.3: The urinary system shouldn't absorb from urine.

scaffolds.q: Transitional epithelium is found in the ___?
scaffolds.explain: Transitional epithelium lines the URINARY BLADDER, URETERS, and URETHRA. These structures need epithelium that can stretch with urine volume changes while maintaining a protective barrier.

wrongExplains.0: Digestive tract has simple columnar for absorption.
wrongExplains.2: Respiratory airways have pseudostratified ciliated columnar.
wrongExplains.3: Blood vessels have simple squamous endothelium.
